title: Turkish Rice Pilaf with Vermicelli (Şehriyeli Pirinç Pilavı)

ingredients.name: basmati or long-grain rice, soaked 15 minutes

subs.name: sona masoori or any long-grain rice, soaked 20 minutes
subs.notes: adjust water by 30 ml if using shorter grain rice

subs.name: fine broken pasta (angel hair) or thin broken spaghetti

ingredients.name: butter
visual_checks: butter gives the best flavour; ghee also works
ingredients.notes: butter gives the best flavour; ghee also works

subs.name: ghee — equal quantity; gives a richer, more Indian character

steps.text: Heat butter and oil together in a heavy-bottomed pot on medium flame until the butter foams and the foam subsides — combined fat prevents the butter from burning. Add the broken vermicelli pieces.
Stir the vermicelli constantly for 3–4 minutes on medium flame until evenly golden-brown and nutty-smelling — watch carefully as they can go from golden to burnt quickly.
Drain the soaked rice well and add to the pot. Stir gently for 1 minute to coat the grains in the butter and lightly toast them.
Pour in 550 ml boiling water (or warm stock) and salt. Stir once, bring to a boil, then reduce to the lowest flame. Cover tightly with a lid.
Cook covered on very low flame for 15 minutes — do not lift the lid. After 15 minutes, remove from heat and leave covered for 10 more minutes. The rice will finish cooking in the residual steam.
Fluff with a fork and serve. Every grain should be separate and lightly golden, with the toasted vermicelli distributed throughout. Serve as a side with kofta, shawarma or any grilled dish.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: butter → ghee — equal quantity; gives a richer, more Indian character (both work beautifully); vermicelli → fine broken pasta (angel hair) or thin broken spaghetti (toast the same way); basmati-rice → sona masoori or any long-grain rice, soaked 20 minutes (adjust water by 30 ml if using shorter grain rice)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Indian seviya (vermicelli) is identical to Turkish şehriye — available in every kirana store for a few rupees. Toast in a kadai with ghee or butter on medium flame. The technique and result are very similar to the rice-and-vermicelli base used in Indian pilaf. Use any long-grain rice you have.
